news_summary: Recent environmental progress includes the discovery of multiple new species within the Himalayan pit viper, innovative sustainability insights from urban ecology, successful conservation efforts boosting the critically endangered New Zealand parakeet, and a significant reduction in deforestation in the Brazilian Amazon.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les récentes avancées en matière d'environnement, on peut citer la découverte de plusieurs nouvelles espèces au sein du genre des vipères de l'Himalaya, des perspectives novatrices en matière de développement durable issues de l'écologie urbaine, le succès des efforts de conservation visant à renforcer les effectifs de la perruche de Nouvelle-Zélande, une espèce en danger critique d'extinction, ainsi qu'une réduction significative de la déforestation en Amazonie brésilienne.
news_summary_es: Entre los avances medioambientales recientes se incluyen el descubrimiento de varias especies nuevas dentro del género de víboras del Himalaya, nuevos conocimientos sobre sostenibilidad procedentes de la ecología urbana, iniciativas de conservación exitosas que han contribuido a la recuperación del periquito de Nueva Zelanda, una especie en peligro crítico de extinción, y una reducción significativa de la deforestación en la Amazonía brasileña.

articles.title: Venomous Himalayan pit viper was actually 5 different species all along
articles.summary: Hidden deep in the towering mountains of the Himalayas, one of Asia’s most mysterious venomous snakes has been keeping a major secret for over 160 years. Scientists have now discovered that the so-called Himalayan pit viper is not just one species, but actually five separate species — including three completely unknown to science until now.
articles.content:
The towering mountain ranges of Asia remain some of the least explored places on Earth when it comes to wildlife. Now, an international team of scientists has discovered that one of the region's most mysterious venomous snakes is not a single species after all. Instead, the Himalayan pit viper group consists of five separate species, including three previously unknown to science.

The findings were published in the open access journal ZooKeys. Researchers combined modern genetic analysis with skeletal studies, physical characteristics, and ecological observations to reexamine the snakes. Their work revealed that the Himalayan pit viper, first described in 1864 and long believed to be a widespread single species, actually represents several deeply distinct evolutionary lineages.

The team identified five species-level lineages in total. These include the Himalayan pit viper in its strict scientific sense, Gloydius chambensis described in 2022, and three newly recognized species found in different regions of Pakistan and Nepal. Along with clear genetic differences, the snakes also showed distinct skeletal and physical traits.

"These mountain systems still harbor overlooked vertebrate diversity and hold important clues to the biogeography of Asia," says Daniel Jablonski of Comenius University Bratislava, who has been conducting extensive research in Pakistan and Afghanistan for many years.

"By combining modern field sampling with data from historical museum specimens, we uncovered evolutionary lineages that had remained hidden for more than a century after the original description of the Himalayan pit viper," said Daniel Jablonski.

Ancient Museum Specimens Help Solve Snake Mystery

One of the most important parts of the study involved DNA taken from museum specimens collected during the 19th and early 20th centuries. Among them was the original type specimen of the Himalayan pit viper, which helped researchers confirm the snake's true scientific identity.

The study highlights the lasting importance of natural history collections. Specimens gathered by explorers generations ago continue to provide valuable scientific insights today, especially in remote regions where fieldwork remains difficult.

In some cases, the evidence had been sitting unnoticed in museums for decades.

"Museum specimens are not just records of the past. They are active research tools and essential infrastructure for future science," says Sylvia Hofmann from the Museum Koenig as part of the Leibniz Institute for the Analysis of Biodiversity Change, who has comprehensively worked in the Himalaya and Tibetan Plateau during the past 20 years.

"Some of the key evidence had been sitting in museum collections for more than a hundred years. We just didn't have the tools to recognize it. As analytical methods continue to improve, the scientific value of these collections will only grow and revealing biodiversity we didn't even know was there," says Sylvia Hofmann.

Why Himalayan Pit Vipers Matter

Herpetofauna, including snakes and other reptiles and amphibians, play important roles in ecosystems. They serve as ecological indicators, predators within food chains, and natural controllers of pest populations. Among the region's top predators are pit vipers adapted to life in rugged mountain environments, though scientists have historically studied them very little in the Himalayas.

"Our work aims to close these gaps in knowledge and to lay the groundwork and provide inspiration for further, in-depth studies on this ecologically and medically relevant group," says Frank Tillack of the Museum für Naturkunde Berlin, Leibniz Institute for Evolution and Biodiversity Research.

Tillack has worked closely with colleagues in Nepal for 35 years on research projects involving Himalayan reptile and amphibian biodiversity.

Remote Mountains Still Hold Biological Surprises

The findings also show how much remains undiscovered in parts of Asia that have been difficult for scientists to access for decades.

"Pakistan's high mountains are still full of biological surprises," says Rafaqat Masroor of the Pakistan Museum of Natural History, one of the country's leading herpetologists.

"This finding highlights how little we still know about a region long shaped by socio-political instability," says Rafaqat Masroor.

Beyond identifying new species, the study also raises important conservation concerns.

"Each of the newly recognized species seems to occupy a relatively restricted range in fragile mountain environments, highlighting new ecological and evolutionary questions," says Daniel Jablonski.

Researchers note that recognizing hidden biodiversity is essential for conservation efforts. Without understanding how many species actually exist, scientists cannot accurately assess threats or create effective plans to protect them.
articles.reasoning: The discovery of three new venomous snake species in the Himalayas represents a significant scientific breakthrough with broad implications for biodiversity knowledge and conservation efforts in a remote, ecologically important region. The study uses advanced genetic and morphological analysis combined with historical museum specimens, highlighting the value of natural history collections and revealing hidden biodiversity. This knowledge can inform conservation strategies and deepen understanding of ecosystem health in fragile mountain environments.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover five distinct species among Himalayan pit vipers, including three new to science
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Himalayan mountains are home to many unknown animals because the area is hard to explore. For over 160 years, scientists thought the venomous Himalayan pit viper was just one species living across this region.

**What happened** – A group of international scientists studied these snakes using modern DNA tests, bone studies, and observations in nature. They found out that what was once one species is actually five different species. Three of these were never known to science before. The team also used old snake samples from museums collected over 100 years ago to help confirm their findings.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that even well-known animals can hide secrets. It also proves how valuable museum collections are for science, as they can reveal new information many years later. Knowing there are five species instead of one helps scientists understand more about the snakes’ evolution and how to protect them. Each species lives in a small mountain area, which means they might need special care to survive.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying these snakes and their habitats to learn more about their roles in the ecosystem. This knowledge will help create better plans to protect these new species and the fragile mountain environments they live in. The discovery may also encourage more exploration of other animals in the Himalayan region.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have revealed that the Himalayan pit viper is actually five species, including three new ones, highlighting hidden biodiversity and the importance of museum specimens in modern research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les vipères des Himalayas sont en fait cinq espèces distinctes, dont trois nouvelles pour la science
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les montagnes de l’Himalaya abritent de nombreux animaux inconnus car la région est difficile à explorer. Pendant plus de 160 ans, les scientifiques pensaient que la vipère des Himalayas était une seule espèce venimeuse répandue dans cette zone.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe internationale de scientifiques a utilisé des tests ADN modernes, des études des os et des observations sur le terrain. Ils ont découvert que la vipère des Himalayas n’est pas une mais cinq espèces différentes. Trois d’entre elles étaient inconnues jusqu’à présent. Ils ont aussi utilisé de vieux spécimens de musée, collectés il y a plus de 100 ans, pour confirmer leurs résultats.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que même des animaux bien connus peuvent cacher des secrets. Elle prouve aussi l’utilité des collections de musées pour la science, qui peuvent révéler de nouvelles informations longtemps après leur collecte. Savoir qu’il y a cinq espèces au lieu d’une aide à mieux comprendre leur évolution et à mieux les protéger. Chaque espèce vit dans une petite zone de montagne, ce qui signifie qu’elles pourraient avoir besoin d’une protection spéciale.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier ces vipères et leurs habitats pour mieux comprendre leur rôle dans l’écosystème. Ces connaissances aideront à créer de meilleurs plans pour protéger ces nouvelles espèces et les environnements fragiles des montagnes. Cette découverte pourrait aussi encourager plus d’explorations d’autres animaux dans l’Himalaya.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont montré que la vipère des Himalayas est en fait cinq espèces, dont trois nouvelles, révélant une biodiversité cachée et l’importance des spécimens de musée dans la recherche moderne.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren que la víbora del Himalaya es en realidad cinco especies diferentes, tres nuevas para la ciencia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las montañas del Himalaya albergan muchos animales desconocidos porque la zona es difícil de explorar. Durante más de 160 años, los científicos pensaron que la víbora venenosa del Himalaya era una sola especie extendida en esta región.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo internacional de científicos usó pruebas modernas de ADN, estudios de esqueletos y observaciones en la naturaleza. Descubrieron que la víbora del Himalaya no es una, sino cinco especies diferentes. Tres de ellas nunca habían sido conocidas por la ciencia. También usaron muestras antiguas de museos, recolectadas hace más de 100 años, para confirmar sus hallazgos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que incluso animales conocidos pueden ocultar secretos. También demuestra el valor de las colecciones de museos para la ciencia, ya que pueden revelar nueva información años después. Saber que hay cinco especies en vez de una ayuda a entender mejor su evolución y a protegerlas. Cada especie vive en una pequeña zona montañosa, lo que significa que podrían necesitar cuidado especial.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando estas víboras y sus hábitats para aprender más sobre su papel en el ecosistema. Esta información ayudará a crear mejores planes para proteger estas nuevas especies y los frágiles ambientes de montaña. El descubrimiento podría motivar más exploraciones de otros animales en el Himalaya.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos revelaron que la víbora del Himalaya es en realidad cinco especies, incluyendo tres nuevas, mostrando biodiversidad oculta y la importancia de los especímenes de museo en la investigación moderna.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of five distinct, colorfully patterned Himalayan pit vipers coiled gracefully on rugged, rocky mountain ledges with subtle skeletal patterns faintly visible beneath their scales, set against a softly lit backdrop of towering Himalayan peaks under a gentle dawn sky, symbolizing discovery and hidden diversity in remote mountain wilderness.

articles.title: Best of Sustainability In Your Ear: Author Nadina Galle on The Nature of Our Cities
articles.summary:
More than half the world’s population—4.4 billion people—live in cities today. That number is expected...
The post Best of Sustainability In Your Ear: Author Nadina Galle on The Nature of Our Cities appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
More than half the world’s population—4.4 billion people—live in cities today. That number is expected to rise to 80% by 2050. Our guest, Nadina Galle, is a trailblazing ecological engineer and author of The Nature of Our Cities. She is an ecological engineer who studies the intersection of nature and technology in urban environments. Nadina developed the concept of an Internet of Nature (IoN) that uses tools like artificial intelligence, automation, and sensors to support and enhance ecosystems within cities. Nadina’s book offers a transformative perspective on how urban spaces can be reimagined in the face of climate change and sprawling development. She shares the inspiring story of the Groene Loper project in Maastricht, Netherlands, where soil sensors were deployed to monitor tree health. The results were remarkable, with trees supported by this technology growing up to three times larger than those without it. This is a powerful example of how technology can not only protect trees but also transform urban spaces into healthier, greener environments.

From fire and the wheel to the reinforced concrete frames that define modern buildings, we are surrounded by technology. We tend to forget that technology emerged in response to nature — too often, we treated nature as the enemy, the chaos to be contained instead of recognizing that nature’s cycles and changes are the harmony we need to join to sustain society. The loss of any semblance of natural patterns, which ultimately leads to the depletion of the resources necessary for life, has inevitably led to the collapse of previous major civilizations. Modern society has more runway than previous societies because we have created a global economy, but that risks an even greater fall for our species when the ecological underpinnings of our prosperity collapse. The Nature of Our Cities, is a powerful, straightforward, and emotionally resonant book to help you think through your role and choices in the restoration of nature. You can find it on Amazon or Powell’s Books.

Subscribe to Sustainability in Your Ear on iTunes and Apple Podcasts.

Follow Sustainability in Your Ear on Spreaker, iHeartRadio, or YouTube.

Editor’s Note: This episode originally aired in December 2024.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant and inspiring ecological engineering approach that uses technology to enhance urban ecosystems, exemplified by the Groene Loper project where sensor technology helped trees grow three times larger. This represents a meaningful advancement in urban sustainability with broad societal and environmental benefits, supported by detailed context and a clear focus on the intersection of nature and technology to combat climate change.
articles.personality_title: How Technology Helps Trees Grow Bigger in Cities
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – More than half of the world’s people, about 4.4 billion, live in cities today. This number is expected to grow to 80% by 2050, making cities very important places for our future. 

**What happened** – Nadina Galle, an ecological engineer and author, studies how nature and technology can work together in cities. She created the idea of the Internet of Nature (IoN), which uses smart tools like sensors and artificial intelligence to help plants and trees grow better. One example is the Groene Loper project in Maastricht, Netherlands, where sensors in the soil helped monitor tree health. Thanks to this technology, trees there grew up to three times bigger than trees without it.

**Impact** – This shows that technology can do more than just build things—it can protect and improve nature in cities. Bigger, healthier trees mean cleaner air, cooler streets, and better places for people and animals. It also offers a new way to fight climate change by making cities greener and more natural.

**What's next step** – Projects like Groene Loper could inspire other cities to use technology to care for their trees and plants. Nadina’s book, The Nature of Our Cities, explains these ideas and encourages people to think about how we can live better with nature in urban areas.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using smart technology like sensors helps trees grow bigger and healthier in cities, showing a new way to bring nature and technology together for a greener future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment la technologie aide les arbres à mieux pousser en ville
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Plus de la moitié de la population mondiale, environ 4,4 milliards de personnes, vivent aujourd’hui en ville. Ce chiffre devrait atteindre 80 % d’ici 2050, ce qui rend les villes très importantes pour notre avenir.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Nadina Galle, ingénieure écologique et auteure, étudie comment la nature et la technologie peuvent travailler ensemble en milieu urbain. Elle a créé le concept d’Internet de la Nature (IoN), qui utilise des outils intelligents comme des capteurs et l’intelligence artificielle pour aider les plantes et les arbres à mieux pousser. Un exemple est le projet Groene Loper à Maastricht, aux Pays-Bas, où des capteurs dans le sol ont permis de surveiller la santé des arbres. Grâce à cette technologie, les arbres ont poussé jusqu’à trois fois plus gros que ceux sans capteurs.

**Impact** – Cela montre que la technologie peut faire plus que construire des choses : elle peut protéger et améliorer la nature en ville. Des arbres plus grands et en meilleure santé signifient un air plus propre, des rues plus fraîches et de meilleurs endroits pour les personnes et les animaux. C’est aussi une nouvelle façon de lutter contre le changement climatique en rendant les villes plus vertes et plus naturelles.

**Prochaine étape** – Des projets comme Groene Loper pourraient inspirer d’autres villes à utiliser la technologie pour prendre soin de leurs arbres et plantes. Le livre de Nadina, La Nature de Nos Villes, explique ces idées et encourage chacun à réfléchir à comment mieux vivre avec la nature en ville.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Utiliser des technologies intelligentes comme les capteurs aide les arbres à devenir plus grands et plus sains en ville, montrant une nouvelle façon d’allier nature et technologie pour un avenir plus vert.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo la tecnología ayuda a que los árboles crezcan más en las ciudades
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Más de la mitad de las personas en el mundo, alrededor de 4.4 mil millones, viven en ciudades hoy en día. Se espera que esta cifra aumente al 80 % para 2050, haciendo que las ciudades sean muy importantes para nuestro futuro.

**Qué pasó** – Nadina Galle, ingeniera ecológica y autora, estudia cómo la naturaleza y la tecnología pueden trabajar juntas en las ciudades. Ella creó la idea del Internet de la Naturaleza (IoN), que usa herramientas inteligentes como sensores e inteligencia artificial para ayudar a las plantas y árboles a crecer mejor. Un ejemplo es el proyecto Groene Loper en Maastricht, Países Bajos, donde sensores en el suelo ayudaron a monitorear la salud de los árboles. Gracias a esta tecnología, los árboles crecieron hasta tres veces más grandes que los que no la usaron.

**Impacto** – Esto muestra que la tecnología puede hacer más que construir cosas: puede proteger y mejorar la naturaleza en las ciudades. Árboles más grandes y saludables significan aire más limpio, calles más frescas y mejores lugares para personas y animales. También ofrece una nueva forma de combatir el cambio climático haciendo que las ciudades sean más verdes y naturales.

**Próximo paso** – Proyectos como Groene Loper podrían inspirar a otras ciudades a usar tecnología para cuidar sus árboles y plantas. El libro de Nadina, La Naturaleza de Nuestras Ciudades, explica estas ideas y anima a las personas a pensar cómo vivir mejor con la naturaleza en las áreas urbanas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usar tecnología inteligente como sensores ayuda a que los árboles crezcan más grandes y saludables en las ciudades, mostrando una nueva forma de unir la naturaleza y la tecnología para un futuro más verde.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a vibrant urban park where large, healthy trees with visible glowing roots intertwine with softly glowing sensor devices embedded in the soil, surrounded by gentle silhouettes of interconnected circuit-like patterns blending into natural elements, all rendered in warm, earthy tones of green, brown, and soft gold.

articles.title: Critically endangered New Zealand parakeet gets lifeline from randy pair
articles.summary: A critically endangered New Zealand parakeet known as kakariki karaka has seen its numbers surge thanks to a pair of super breeders now responsible for more than 10 percent of the total population. Parents Nacho and Trixie were paired up in 2024 at the Isaac Conservation and Wildlife Trust in Christchurch and have since produced 55 chicks, including 33 this year alone.
articles.content:
Nacho – part of a breeding pair of the rare New Zealand kakariki karaka parakeets – has produced 55 chicks in two years, more than 10% of the total population of the critically endangered bird.

One of the world's rarest parakeets has seen its numbers surge thanks to a pair of super breeders now responsible for more than 10 percent of the total population.

The New Zealand native kakariki karaka – or orange-fronted parakeet – is critically endangered and has twice been declared extinct, only to be rediscovered.

There are around 450 of the birds left, mainly in sanctuaries and predator-free islands but also in wild populations.

Parents Nacho and Trixie were paired up in 2024 at the Isaac Conservation and Wildlife Trust in Christchurch and have since produced 55 chicks, including 33 this year alone.

Wildlife manager Leigh Percasky praised "super-mum" Trixie.

"The breeding season has ended and yet she's still producing eggs and raising chicks," Percasky said.

Read more'It's blue!': Scientists discover new deep-sea octopus near Galapagos Islands

"Ideally we'd prefer her to stop so she can have a rest, but she shows no signs of that with another seven chicks in her most recent clutch.

"Nacho also deserves credit as he's responsible for finding food for both Trixie and the chicks which is incredibly busy."

Captive breeding

Wayne Beggs, lead of the Department of Conservation's kakariki karaka recovery programme, said breeding pairs like Nacho and Trixie were ensuring the species didn't go extinct.

"We rely on the captive breeding programmes as without them we couldn't establish new sites," Beggs said.

"The wild populations are very vulnerable to predators, so we always need backup populations."

Percasky said Nacho and Trixie had made a "massive contribution" to the survival of their species, but he wants the love birds to "have a well-earned break" after their latest clutch.

"I'm not sure where they get all their energy from."

(FRANCE 24 with AFP)
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant positive impact on the conservation of a critically endangered species, the New Zealand kakariki karaka parakeet, through successful captive breeding efforts. The story highlights a meaningful increase in the population due to a breeding pair responsible for over 10% of the total population, which is a substantial contribution to species recovery. The article provides sufficient context about the species' endangered status, the breeding program, and the broader conservation efforts, making it an inspiring environmental success story with broad ecological significance.
articles.personality_title: Rare New Zealand parakeet population boosted by dedicated breeding pair
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The kakariki karaka, also called the orange-fronted parakeet, is a rare bird native to New Zealand. It is critically endangered, with only about 450 left. These birds live mostly in protected areas to keep them safe from predators.

**What happened** – At the Isaac Conservation and Wildlife Trust in Christchurch, a pair of kakariki karaka named Nacho and Trixie have helped increase the bird’s numbers. Since being paired in 2024, they have raised 55 chicks together. This includes 33 chicks born this year alone, which is more than 10% of the entire population.

**Impact** – This pair’s success is important because it helps prevent the species from disappearing. The kakariki karaka has faced extinction twice before, so every new chick is a step toward saving the species. Their efforts also support breeding programs that create safe places for more birds to live.

**What’s next step** – Conservation teams will continue to care for Nacho and Trixie and use the chicks they raise to strengthen wild populations. They hope to balance the birds’ health by giving the parents rest after busy breeding seasons. The goal is to keep growing the population and eventually return more birds safely to the wild.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A special parakeet pair is helping save their species by producing over 10% of the total kakariki karaka population through careful breeding efforts.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une paire de perruches rare de Nouvelle-Zélande booste la population en danger critique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le kakariki karaka, ou perruche à front orange, est un oiseau rare originaire de Nouvelle-Zélande. Il est en danger critique d’extinction, avec seulement environ 450 individus restants. Ces oiseaux vivent surtout dans des zones protégées pour les préserver des prédateurs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – À la Isaac Conservation and Wildlife Trust de Christchurch, un couple de kakariki karaka nommé Nacho et Trixie a contribué à augmenter le nombre d’oiseaux. Depuis leur mise en couple en 2024, ils ont élevé ensemble 55 poussins, dont 33 cette année, soit plus de 10 % de la population totale.

**Impact** – Le succès de ce couple est important car il aide à empêcher l’extinction de l’espèce. Le kakariki karaka a déjà disparu deux fois, donc chaque nouveau poussin est un pas vers la sauvegarde. Leur travail soutient aussi les programmes d’élevage qui créent des refuges sûrs pour d’autres oiseaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les équipes de conservation continueront de s’occuper de Nacho et Trixie et d’utiliser leurs poussins pour renforcer les populations sauvages. Elles espèrent aussi ménager les parents après leurs saisons de reproduction intenses. L’objectif est de faire grandir la population et de relâcher plus d’oiseaux en liberté.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un couple spécial de perruches aide à sauver son espèce en produisant plus de 10 % de la population totale de kakariki karaka grâce à un élevage soigné.
articles.personality_title_es: Pareja de periquitos raros de Nueva Zelanda impulsa la población en peligro crítico
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El kakariki karaka, también llamado periquito de frente naranja, es un ave rara nativa de Nueva Zelanda. Está en peligro crítico de extinción, con solo unos 450 ejemplares restantes. Estos pájaros viven principalmente en áreas protegidas para mantenerse a salvo de depredadores.

**Qué pasó** – En el Isaac Conservation and Wildlife Trust en Christchurch, una pareja de kakariki karaka llamada Nacho y Trixie ha ayudado a aumentar el número de aves. Desde que se emparejaron en 2024, han criado 55 polluelos juntos, incluyendo 33 nacidos solo este año, que es más del 10 % de la población total.

**Impacto** – El éxito de esta pareja es importante porque ayuda a evitar que la especie desaparezca. El kakariki karaka ha enfrentado la extinción dos veces antes, por lo que cada nuevo polluelo es un paso para salvar la especie. Sus esfuerzos también apoyan programas de cría que crean lugares seguros para más aves.

**Próximo paso** – Los equipos de conservación seguirán cuidando de Nacho y Trixie y usarán los polluelos que críen para fortalecer las poblaciones salvajes. Esperan equilibrar la salud de los padres dándoles descanso después de temporadas de cría muy activas. La meta es seguir aumentando la población y devolver más aves a la naturaleza.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una pareja especial de periquitos está ayudando a salvar su especie al producir más del 10 % de la población total de kakariki karaka mediante esfuerzos cuidadosos de cría.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant orange-fronted kakariki parakeet pair perched closely together on a delicate branch, surrounded by a nest filled with numerous small, lively chicks, set against a softly colored natural sanctuary background that reflects hope and renewal.

articles.title: Deforestation in Brazilian Amazon falls to lowest level since 2019
articles.summary: A report published Wednesday said that deforestation in the Brazilian Amazon fell last year to its lowest level since 2019, in what will be seen as good news for leftist President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva. Lula, who is seeking a fourth term in October, has made the fight against deforestation a central tenet of his administration.
articles.content:
An aerial view of a field fire, in Sao Felix do Xingu, Para state, Brazil, on June 16, 2025.

Deforestation in the Brazilian Amazon fell last year to its lowest level since 2019, according to a report published Wednesday that will be seen as good news for leftist President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva.

South America's biggest country lost 985,000 hectares (2.4 million acres) of native vegetation last year, down 20.6 percent from 2024, the MapBiomas monitoring network announced.

The figure is the lowest since the network began keeping records in 2019.

It notably does not include forest lost to fires, but after a record fire season in 2024, the country was relatively spared major infernos last year.

Lula, who is seeking a fourth term in October elections, has made the fight against deforestation a central tenet of his administration.

Read moreProtections for emperor penguins in focus as Antarctica talks start in Japan

Preserving forest cover is essential to fighting climate warming as trees act as a natural carbon sink.

After four years of widespread logging under his far-right predecessor Jair Bolsonaro, Lula has pledged to eradicate illegal deforestation altogether by 2030.

The reduction in deforestation was noted across Brazil's six major ecosystems.

"We are seeing an increase in enforcement actions and sanctions (...) which have a direct correlation with the drop in deforestation in all Brazilian biomes," Marcos Rosa, MapBiomas's technical coordinator, told AFP.

Five trees felled per second

Even so, the rate of destruction remains breathtaking.

In the Amazon, the world's largest rainforest, where deforestation slowed by 23.5 percent, five trees are still felled every second.

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again DOWN TO EARTH © FRANCE 24 03:59

The hardest-hit biome last year was once again the Cerrado, a vast, biodiverse savanna south of the Amazon.

It alone accounted for more than half of the deforestation.

MapBiomas – a consortium of universities, NGOs and technology companies – said agriculture accounted for 99 percent of vegetation loss.

Lula is keen to showcase his environmental achievements ahead of the election.

Last year, he hosted the COP30 climate summit in the Amazonian city of Belem.

He has however been criticized by environmentalists for his support of a massive oil exploration project near the mouth of the Amazon River.

(FRANCE 24 with AFP)
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant reduction in deforestation in the Brazilian Amazon to its lowest level since 2019, reflecting meaningful progress in environmental conservation with broad implications for climate change mitigation. It highlights government enforcement actions and policy focus under President Lula, showing a tangible positive impact on a critical global ecosystem. The story is focused, detailed, and covers a large-scale environmental issue affecting biodiversity and carbon sequestration.
articles.personality_title: Deforestation in Brazilian Amazon drops to lowest level since 2019
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Amazon rainforest in Brazil is one of the largest and most important forests on Earth. It helps fight climate change by storing carbon and is home to many plants and animals. But in recent years, parts of the forest have been cut down or destroyed, which is called deforestation.

**What happened** – In 2025, deforestation in the Brazilian Amazon fell to its lowest level since 2019. The MapBiomas network, which tracks changes in land use, reported that Brazil lost 985,000 hectares (about 2.4 million acres) of native vegetation in 2025. This is 20.6% less than the previous year. The reduction was seen across all major ecosystems in Brazil, especially the Amazon rainforest where deforestation dropped by 23.5%.

**Impact** – This drop in deforestation is important because it means fewer trees are being cut down, which helps protect the environment and fight climate change. Trees absorb carbon dioxide, a gas that causes global warming. The decrease also shows that government efforts to stop illegal logging and protect forests are having a positive effect. However, deforestation is still happening fast, with about five trees cut every second in the Amazon.

**What's next step** – President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, who made fighting deforestation a key part of his government, aims to stop illegal deforestation completely by 2030. Continued monitoring and enforcement will be important to keep reducing forest loss. The government will also need to balance protecting the forest with other activities like farming and oil exploration.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Brazil’s efforts have led to the biggest drop in Amazon deforestation since 2019, showing progress in protecting this vital forest.
articles.personality_title_fr: La déforestation dans l’Amazonie brésilienne atteint son plus bas niveau depuis 2019
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La forêt amazonienne au Brésil est l’une des plus grandes et importantes forêts du monde. Elle aide à lutter contre le changement climatique en stockant du carbone et abrite de nombreuses plantes et animaux. Mais ces dernières années, une partie de la forêt a été coupée ou détruite, ce qu’on appelle la déforestation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, la déforestation dans l’Amazonie brésilienne a chuté à son plus bas niveau depuis 2019. Le réseau MapBiomas, qui suit les changements d’utilisation des terres, a rapporté que le Brésil a perdu 985 000 hectares (environ 2,4 millions d’acres) de végétation native en 2025. C’est 20,6 % de moins que l’année précédente. Cette baisse a été observée dans tous les principaux écosystèmes du Brésil, en particulier dans la forêt amazonienne où la déforestation a diminué de 23,5 %.

**Impact** – Cette baisse est importante car elle signifie que moins d’arbres sont coupés, ce qui aide à protéger l’environnement et à lutter contre le changement climatique. Les arbres absorbent le dioxyde de carbone, un gaz qui réchauffe la planète. Cette diminution montre aussi que les efforts du gouvernement pour stopper la coupe illégale et protéger les forêts portent leurs fruits. Cependant, la déforestation continue à un rythme rapide, avec environ cinq arbres coupés chaque seconde dans l’Amazonie.

**Prochaine étape** – Le président Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, qui a fait de la lutte contre la déforestation une priorité, vise à éliminer complètement la déforestation illégale d’ici 2030. Il sera important de continuer la surveillance et les actions pour réduire encore la perte de forêt. Le gouvernement devra aussi trouver un équilibre entre la protection de la forêt et d’autres activités comme l’agriculture et l’exploration pétrolière.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Les efforts du Brésil ont permis la plus forte baisse de la déforestation en Amazonie depuis 2019, montrant des progrès dans la protection de cette forêt essentielle.
articles.personality_title_es: La deforestación en la Amazonía brasileña cae a su nivel más bajo desde 2019
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La selva amazónica en Brasil es una de las más grandes e importantes del mundo. Ayuda a combatir el cambio climático al almacenar carbono y es hogar de muchas plantas y animales. Pero en los últimos años, partes del bosque han sido cortadas o destruidas, lo que se llama deforestación.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, la deforestación en la Amazonía brasileña cayó a su nivel más bajo desde 2019. La red MapBiomas, que monitorea los cambios en el uso de la tierra, informó que Brasil perdió 985,000 hectáreas (alrededor de 2.4 millones de acres) de vegetación nativa en 2025. Esto es un 20.6% menos que el año anterior. La reducción se vio en todos los principales ecosistemas de Brasil, especialmente en la selva amazónica donde la deforestación bajó un 23.5%.

**Impacto** – Esta caída en la deforestación es importante porque significa que se están cortando menos árboles, lo que ayuda a proteger el medio ambiente y a combatir el cambio climático. Los árboles absorben dióxido de carbono, un gas que causa el calentamiento global. La disminución también muestra que los esfuerzos del gobierno para detener la tala ilegal y proteger los bosques están teniendo un efecto positivo. Sin embargo, la deforestación sigue ocurriendo rápidamente, con unos cinco árboles cortados cada segundo en la Amazonía.

**Próximo paso** – El presidente Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, quien hizo de la lucha contra la deforestación una prioridad, busca eliminar la deforestación ilegal por completo para 2030. Será importante continuar con la vigilancia y las acciones para seguir reduciendo la pérdida de bosque. El gobierno también tendrá que equilibrar la protección del bosque con otras actividades como la agricultura y la exploración petrolera.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los esfuerzos de Brasil lograron la mayor reducción en la deforestación de la Amazonía desde 2019, mostrando avances en la protección de este bosque vital.
